ontology.foodex2_gap_note: FoodEx2 does not have a dedicated stout code; A00LQ (beer, dark) is the closest defensible anchor under the alcoholic beverages hierarchy.
ontology.snomed_ct_gap_note: SNOMED CT 53410008 covers beer as a substance class; no stout-specific code exists.

serving.natural_language_portion: One standard 330 ml can or bottle of stout, equivalent to approximately 330 g, is a typical single serving for an adult.

nutrient_source.title: Beer, stout — HPB Singapore Food Composition Database
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: Input record carries HPB provenance at confidence 0.95 for all per_100g fields. Values are accepted as-is. Supplementary micronutrient data cross-referenced with USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy entry for beer, dark (FDC ID 168746) to populate fields not reported by HPB.

gi_source.title: International tables of glycemic index and glycemic load values: 2008 — Foster-Powell K, Holt SHA, Brand-Miller JC
gi_source.organization: American Journal of Clinical Nutrition (peer-reviewed journal)
gi_source.source_match_notes: Beer GI of approximately 66 (medium) is reported in the 2008 International GI Tables (Foster-Powell et al., Am J Clin Nutr 2008) for beer as a beverage category. Stout is a dark beer variant; no stout-specific GI entry exists in the University of Sydney GI Database or peer-reviewed literature. The beer GI value is used as the closest defensible analogous estimate. Glycemic load per serving calculated as: GL = (GI × carbohydrate_g per serving) / 100 = (66 × 29.04) / 100 ≈ 19.2, where carbohydrate per 330 g serving = 8.8 × 3.3 = 29.04 g.

hypertension.key_factor: Alcohol content raises blood pressure acutely and chronically; interacts with antihypertensive medications
hypertension.guidance: Individuals with hypertension should avoid stout and all alcoholic beverages, as even moderate alcohol consumption raises systolic blood pressure acutely and chronic intake undermines antihypertensive therapy.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Alcohol impairs hepatic glucose regulation and increases hypoglycaemia risk; carbohydrate load from malted barley contributes to glycaemic burden
type2_diabetes.guidance: Individuals with Type 2 Diabetes should avoid stout because alcohol impairs hepatic glucose output, increases hypoglycaemia risk in those on insulin or sulfonylureas, and the 8.8 g/100 g carbohydrate content from malted barley adds to glycaemic load.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Alcohol is a direct cardiotoxin at regular intake levels; raises triglycerides and blood pressure; interacts with anticoagulant and antiplatelet medications
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: Individuals with cardiovascular disease should avoid stout because regular alcohol consumption raises triglycerides, elevates blood pressure, promotes arrhythmia risk, and interacts with anticoagulant and antiplatelet medications commonly used in CVD management.

rheumatoid_arthritis.key_factor: Alcohol is pro-inflammatory and contraindicated with methotrexate and most RA biologics due to hepatotoxicity risk
rheumatoid_arthritis.guidance: Individuals with rheumatoid arthritis should avoid stout because alcohol is directly pro-inflammatory and is contraindicated with methotrexate and most biologic therapies used in RA management due to increased hepatotoxicity risk.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Stout is a dark, full-bodied beer brewed from roasted barley or roasted malt, water, hops, and yeast. The roasting process gives stout its characteristic near-black colour, bitter-sweet flavour profile, and notes of coffee, chocolate, and caramel. Common styles include dry stout (e.g., Guinness), sweet or milk stout (brewed with lactose), oatmeal stout, and imperial stout. Stout originated in the British Isles and is now consumed globally, including in Singapore where it is widely available in bars, restaurants, and retail. Per 100 g, stout provides approximately 52 kcal, 0.2 g protein, 0.0 g fat, and 8.8 g carbohydrate, with a low sodium content of 4 mg. The carbohydrate derives primarily from residual fermentable sugars and dextrins from malted barley. Stout contains alcohol (typically 4–8% ABV), which is the primary clinical concern across all health conditions. Alcohol impairs hepatic glucose regulation, raises blood pressure, elevates triglycerides, is pro-inflammatory, and interacts with a wide range of medications including antihypertensives, antidiabetic agents, anticoagulants, and immunosuppressants. Stout is not suitable for individuals managing hypertension, Type 2 Diabetes, cardiovascular disease, or rheumatoid arthritis. It is not halal and contains gluten from barley, making it unsuitable for those with coeliac disease or gluten intolerance.
common_user_queries: How many calories in stout beer?
Can I drink stout if I have high blood pressure?
What is the difference between stout and porter?

confidence_reason: Core macronutrient values sourced directly from HPB Singapore Food Composition Database at confidence 0.95. Supplementary micronutrient values cross-referenced with USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy (beer, dark). GI estimated from peer-reviewed International GI Tables (Foster-Powell et al., 2008) as no stout-specific GI entry exists; estimation basis documented.
notes: fat_g is confirmed zero per HPB source; stout is a fermented grain beverage with negligible fat content by formulation. saturated_fat_g, monounsaturated_fat_g, polyunsaturated_fat_g, trans_fat_g, and cholesterol_mg are confirmed zero per USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy (beer, dark, FDC ID 168746) — beer contains no dietary fat fractions. sugar_g is confirmed zero per HPB source; residual fermentable sugars in dry stout are below detection threshold. dietary_fibre_g is confirmed zero per HPB source; filtration during brewing removes fibre. calcium_mg and iron_mg are reported as 0.0 by HPB; cross-reference with USDA SR Legacy confirms trace/negligible amounts below reporting threshold. GI value of 66 is an analogous estimate from the 2008 International GI Tables for beer as a category; no stout-specific GI entry exists in the University of Sydney GI Database. The input record incorrectly assigned primary_cuisine as 'chinese' and ethnic_tags as ['chinese']; stout is a Western/International beverage of Irish/British origin — corrected in this output. The input dietary_tags 'low_sodium' and 'low_calorie' are factually accurate per nutrient values but are not clinically meaningful given the alcohol content; these tags are not carried forward as dietary_flags given the alcohol contraindications. Vegan flag set to false as some stout variants (milk stout) use lactose; the generic stout record cannot guarantee vegan status. monounsaturated_fat_g, polyunsaturated_fat_g, trans_fat_g set to 0.0 (confirmed zero per USDA SR Legacy for beer, dark); not -1 as the source explicitly reports these values.
